title: What is a 'Controlled Foreign Corporation' (CFC) for a US Shareholder?

interlinking_suggestions.title: The Ultimate Digital Nomad Tax Survival Guide for 2025
How to Create a Financial Model for Your Freelance Business

author.name: Ben Carter
author.bio: A certified financial planner specializing in the unique challenges faced by US citizens abroad. Ben's articles provide actionable advice on everything from FBAR and FATCA compliance to retirement planning for expats.

reviewedBy.name: Dr. Alistair Finch
reviewedBy.bio: With a Ph.D. in Economics and over 15 years at a Big Four accounting firm, Alistair specializes in demystifying cross-border tax law for independent professionals. He focuses on risk mitigation and long-term financial planning.

key_takeaways: Determine if you are a U.S. person owning 10% or more of a foreign corporation, as this status automatically triggers complex international tax obligations.
Recognize that your CFC's active business profits are likely subject to immediate U.S. tax under the GILTI regime, even if the funds are never distributed to you personally.
Assess your total ownership by including shares held by your spouse, children, and parents, as these 'attribution rules' can unexpectedly make you a CFC owner.
Prioritize the annual filing of the complex Form 5471 to avoid severe, automatic penalties that begin at $10,000 per year for non-compliance.
summary: Many U.S. global professionals unknowingly own a Controlled Foreign Corporation (CFC), exposing their foreign profits to immediate U.S. taxation and risking severe penalties for non-compliance. The core advice is to first diagnose your CFC status, understand that your active income is likely taxable under GILTI rules, and then engage a professional to handle the mandatory Form 5471 filing. By taking these steps, you can transform compliance anxiety into a clear action plan, avoiding costly fines and managing your global business with confidence.
ai_overview_answer: A foreign company is considered a Controlled Foreign Corporation (CFC) when U.S. persons who each own at least 10% of the company collectively own more than 50% of its vote or value. For a U.S. citizen or resident who is the sole owner of their foreign business, the entity is definitively a CFC. As a CFC owner, you are required to pay U.S. tax on certain types of the company's income in the year it is earned, even if the money is not distributed to you. This income typically falls into two categories: Subpart F for passive earnings and Global Intangible Low-Taxed Income (GILTI) for most active business profits. Determining ownership requires considering "constructive ownership" rules, where stock held by certain family members may be attributed to you. The primary compliance obligation is to file Form 5471, an annual information return, with your personal U.S. tax return. Failure to file this complex form correctly can result in significant penalties, starting at $10,000 per year. Due to the complexity, engaging a U.S. international tax professional is critical for accurate reporting and risk mitigation.

itemListElement.name: What is a 'Controlled Foreign Corporation' (CFC) for a US Shareholder?

@graph.name: How to Determine and Manage Your Controlled Foreign Corporation (CFC) Status
@graph.headline: What is a 'Controlled Foreign Corporation' (CFC) for a US Shareholder?
@graph.description: Are you an accidental owner of a controlled foreign corporation (CFC)? Our 3-step playbook helps you diagnose your status, map risks, and tame Form 5471.

step.name: The 60-Second Diagnosis
step.text: Answer three key questions to determine if your foreign entity is a CFC: 1. Are you a 'U.S. Person' for tax purposes? 2. Do you own 10% or more of a foreign corporation? 3. Do U.S. Shareholders collectively own more than 50% of the company? If you answer 'Yes' to all three, you own a CFC.

step.name: Mapping Your Risk with Subpart F & GILTI
step.text: Understand the two main types of income the IRS targets: Subpart F for passive income (like interest and dividends) and GILTI for most active business profits (like consulting fees). For a service business in a low-tax country, GILTI is the most significant risk. Also, be aware of 'constructive ownership' rules, where shares owned by family members can be attributed to you, potentially triggering CFC status unexpectedly.

step.name: Your Action Plan: Taming Form 5471
step.text: The primary compliance action is filing Form 5471, an information return attached to your personal tax return. Ignoring this form leads to automatic penalties starting at $10,000 per year. Gather your company's financial statements and ownership details, and engage a qualified tax professional to prepare this complex filing to mitigate risk.

mainEntity.name: How do I know if my foreign company is a CFC?

acceptedAnswer.text: A foreign company becomes a CFC when 'U.S. Shareholders' (U.S. persons owning at least 10% each) collectively own more than 50% of it. If you are a U.S. person and the sole owner of your foreign corporation, it is a CFC. You cross both the 10% and 50% thresholds by yourself.

mainEntity.name: Do CFC rules apply to small businesses or freelancers?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes, absolutely. The rules are triggered by ownership percentages, not revenue or company size. A U.S. freelance consultant who is the 100% owner of their foreign corporation is the classic example of a CFC owner. There is no small business exemption.

mainEntity.name: What is the difference between Subpart F and GILTI for a consultant?

acceptedAnswer.text: Think of them as two nets. Subpart F is an older net that catches passive income (interest, dividends) or income from specific related-party transactions. GILTI is a newer, broader net that catches most active business income (like your consulting fees) that isn't already subject to a high foreign tax rate. For a consultant in a low-tax country, your primary income will almost certainly fall under GILTI.

mainEntity.name: What is the main reporting requirement for a CFC?

acceptedAnswer.text: The primary obligation is filing IRS Form 5471 annually. This information return must be attached to your personal or business tax return and provides the IRS with a detailed look at the foreign corporation's finances. Failure to file correctly can lead to penalties starting at $10,000 per year.

mainEntity.name: Can a foreign LLC be a CFC?

acceptedAnswer.text: It depends on its U.S. tax election. By default, a foreign single-member LLC is 'disregarded' and cannot be a CFC. However, if that LLC makes an election with the IRS to be treated as a corporation (a 'check-the-box' election), then it absolutely can be a CFC if the ownership tests are met.

mainEntity.name: I just realized I own a CFC and haven't been filing. What should I do?

acceptedAnswer.text: First, do not panic. This is a common situation. The key is to be proactive. Immediately seek guidance from a qualified U.S. international tax professional. They can help you access programs like the IRS's Streamlined Filing Compliance Procedures, which are designed for taxpayers whose non-compliance was non-willful and allow you to get caught up, often with reduced or waived penalties.

body.content: The Accidental CFC Owner: Your 3-Step Playbook for Compliance & Peace of Mind

Before diving into dense regulations, this playbook begins with a simple, powerful diagnostic. The term **Controlled Foreign Corporation (CFC)** was created to prevent U.S. tax deferral by holding profits in low-tax countries, but its modern definition often ensnares solo global professionals. The first step toward confident compliance is to clarify your status.
Let’s cut through the noise with a straightforward, three-part checklist. If you answer "Yes" to all three questions, you are very likely the owner of a CFC.
Step 1: The 60-Second Diagnosis
This is the foundational question: Does this even apply to you? A CFC is a technical definition based on a few clear ownership tests.

items:
**Question 1: Are you a "U.S. Person" for tax purposes?**
This is the anchor for all U.S. international tax matters. The IRS definition of a **U.S. Person** is broad and has nothing to do with where you live. It includes U.S. citizens (even if you've lived abroad your entire life), lawful permanent residents (Green Card holders), and any individual who meets the “substantial presence test” based on days physically in the United States. If you fit any of these categories, your global income is subject to U.S. taxation, and you've met the first condition.

**Question 2: Do you own 10% or more of a foreign corporation?**
If the answer is yes, you have a specific designation under the tax code: you are a **"U.S. Shareholder."** This isn't a casual term; it's a legal trigger. The test considers 10% or more of either the total voting power or the total value of the corporation's stock. Even if you hold non-voting shares, if their value represents a tenth or more of the company, you cross this threshold. For the solo professional who owns their entire foreign entity, the answer is an unequivocal "Yes."

**Question 3: Do U.S. Shareholders collectively own more than 50% of the company?**
This is the "control" part of the CFC definition. The IRS checks if U.S. Shareholders (as defined above) collectively own more than half of the company's vote or value. While this can be complex in a multi-shareholder company, it’s brutally simple for a "Business-of-One." If you are a U.S. Person owning 100% of your foreign company, you are both the 10% U.S. Shareholder and the 50%+ controlling owner. You satisfy all conditions by yourself.

body.content: **The Verdict:** If you just answered "Yes" three times, take a deep breath. This is not a tax problem; it's a tax *reality*. You have successfully diagnosed your business as a Controlled Foreign Corporation. This clarity is your greatest asset. It simply means a specific set of U.S. compliance obligations now apply to you. Instead of operating with uncertainty, you have a clear direction to manage your responsibilities.
Step 2: Mapping Your Risk with Subpart F & GILTI

Now that you know you're operating a CFC, you can shift from worrying about the unknown to strategically managing your obligations. The core of CFC compliance is this: the IRS requires you, the U.S. Shareholder, to pay U.S. tax on certain types of your company's income *in the year it is earned*—even if you never transfer a dollar to your personal bank account.
This income primarily falls into two buckets: **Subpart F income** and Global Intangible Low-Taxed Income (**GILTI**).
Subpart F: The "Passive and Easily Moved" Money

Think of Subpart F as the original anti-deferral rule, enacted in 1962. It targets income that is passive or can be easily shifted to low-tax jurisdictions. For a solo global professional, this typically includes:

items: **Passive Income:** The most straightforward category, covering dividends, interest earned on corporate bank accounts, rents, and royalties.

**Foreign Base Company Income:** This sounds complex, but it’s designed to stop you from using your foreign company to artificially route sales or services. For instance, if your UAE-based company provides consulting services to a related company in the United States, the income could be considered a specific type of Subpart F income.

body.content: GILTI: The "Catch-All" for Your Active Business Profits

The GILTI regime, introduced in 2017, is the newer, much broader rule that catches almost everything else. It acts as a global minimum tax on the profits of a CFC. While its name refers to "intangible" income, its practical application is far wider, covering most active business earnings that are not already subject to a high rate of foreign tax.

For a service-based "Business-of-One" in a low or zero-tax country, GILTI is the most significant risk. That $150,000 in consulting profit your business earns is the exact type of income the GILTI rules are designed to tax.
To simplify the distinction:

rows: Primarily passive income (dividends, interest) and specific related-party transactions.
A broad category of active business income not otherwise taxed at a high rate.
How much foreign tax was paid on the active income?
Your CFC earns interest on its corporate savings account.
Your consulting business in a 0% tax country earns its main operating profit.

body.content: The Hidden Trap: Why "Ownership" Is More Than Your Name on the Shares

Understanding your income buckets is critical, but this analysis can be undermined by a hidden trap in the tax code: the rules of "constructive ownership." The IRS looks beyond share certificates to determine who truly controls a corporation, meaning you can be legally treated as owning stock that is actually held by your family.
These "attribution" rules prevent taxpayers from sidestepping CFC status by dividing ownership among relatives. For the global professional, this isn't a technicality; it's a direct threat.

items: **Family Attribution:** This is the most common snare. The IRS treats stock owned by your spouse, children, grandchildren, and parents as if you owned it yourself. Imagine you own 5% of a foreign tech company. Your spouse, who is not a U.S. person, owns another 5%. In your mind, you are safely below the 10% U.S. Shareholder threshold. However, the IRS attributes your spouse's shares to you, making you a 10% owner and instantly triggering complex CFC obligations.

**Entity Attribution:** Ownership can also flow up from partnerships or trusts. If you are a partner in a firm that owns stock in a foreign corporation, the IRS considers you to own a proportional share of that stock.

body.content: These rules are a minefield. An innocent gift of stock to a child or a business partnership with a parent could inadvertently create a CFC. You must proactively map your *entire* ownership structure—including shares held by family and related entities—to see the full picture as the IRS sees it. This is a fundamental exercise in risk management.
Step 3: Your Action Plan: Taming Form 5471

Navigating ownership rules is a crucial defensive step, but it’s incomplete without the critical offensive action: filing Form 5471. For CFC owners, this form is the absolute centerpiece of your compliance obligation, and ignoring it carries severe consequences.

**Form 5471, *Information Return of U.S. Persons With Respect to Certain Foreign Corporations*, is an *information return*, not a tax bill.** You attach it to your personal U.S. tax return (Form 1040) to give the IRS a transparent look into your foreign entity's financial activities, including its balance sheet and income statement.

The penalties for non-compliance should command your full attention. Failure to file triggers an **automatic $10,000 penalty** for each form that is late, incomplete, or incorrect—per year. If the failure continues after IRS notification, additional penalties can accrue up to $50,000. This is the single greatest financial risk for an unaware CFC owner.
You can transform this anxiety into action. While the form is notoriously complex, preparation begins with gathering the right information:

items: **Complete Financial Statements:** A proper profit and loss statement and a balance sheet for your foreign corporation.
**Transactional Records:** Details of any transactions between you (the shareholder) and the corporation.
**Verified Ownership Structure:** A precise calculation of your ownership percentage, including any shares attributed to you from family members.

body.content: Form 5471 is widely considered one of the most complex filings in the U.S. tax code. The IRS estimates it can take over 30 hours to complete. Engaging a professional is an investment in risk mitigation, not a discretionary expense. As CPA Kristal Sepulveda notes, a common misconception is that U.S. tax obligations don't follow citizens abroad, but "this misconception can lead to significant penalties and interest charges." Her point is acutely true for the specialist requirements of Form 5471. Navigating this alone is like performing surgery on yourself—the risk of a catastrophic mistake is simply too high.
Frequently Asked Questions

items:
**How do I know if my foreign company is a CFC?**
A foreign company becomes a CFC when "U.S. Shareholders" (U.S. persons owning at least 10% each) collectively own more than 50% of it. If you are a U.S. person and the sole owner of your foreign corporation, it is a CFC. You cross both the 10% and 50% thresholds by yourself.

**Do CFC rules apply to small businesses or freelancers?**
Yes, absolutely. The rules are triggered by ownership percentages, not revenue or company size. A U.S. freelance consultant who is the 100% owner of their foreign corporation is the classic example of a CFC owner. There is no small business exemption.

**What is the difference between Subpart F and GILTI for a consultant?**
Think of them as two nets. **Subpart F** is an older net that catches passive income (interest, dividends) or income from specific related-party transactions. **GILTI** is a newer, broader net that catches most active business income (like your consulting fees) that isn't already subject to a high foreign tax rate. For a consultant in a low-tax country, your primary income will almost certainly fall under GILTI.

**What is the main reporting requirement for a CFC?**
The primary obligation is filing IRS **Form 5471** annually. This information return must be attached to your personal or business tax return and provides the IRS with a detailed look at the foreign corporation's finances. Failure to file correctly can lead to penalties starting at $10,000 per year.

**Can a foreign LLC be a CFC?**
It depends on its U.S. tax election. By default, a foreign single-member LLC is "disregarded" and cannot be a CFC. However, if that LLC makes an election with the IRS to be treated as a corporation (a "check-the-box" election), then it absolutely can be a CFC if the ownership tests are met.

**I just realized I own a CFC and haven't been filing. What should I do?**
First, do not panic. This is a common situation. The key is to be proactive. Immediately seek guidance from a qualified U.S. international tax professional. They can help you access programs like the IRS's Streamlined Filing Compliance Procedures, which are designed for taxpayers whose non-compliance was non-willful and allow you to get caught up, often with reduced or waived penalties.

body.content: From Anxiety to Action: Owning Your Global Compliance

The CFC rules are not an insurmountable threat once you understand the playbook. By working through this guide, you have transformed an "unknown unknown" into a concrete business process. You’ve diagnosed your status, mapped your primary risks under Subpart F and GILTI, and identified your most critical action: filing Form 5471.

This clarity is the ultimate antidote to compliance anxiety. The goal was never for you to become an international tax expert, but to empower you to engage one from a position of knowledge. Instead of approaching a professional with a vague sense of dread, you are now prepared to lead a productive, specific conversation about your global business.
Your next step is to leverage this knowledge. You are no longer a passive recipient of information; you are a strategic partner in your own compliance.
**Your New Conversation Starter with a Tax Professional:**

items: "I've determined my foreign company is a CFC. Can we confirm this and walk through the specific implications for my business model?"
"My primary revenue is active consulting fees in a low-tax country, so I believe my main exposure is to GILTI, not Subpart F. Is that correct, and can you model my potential U.S. tax liability?"
"What is your specific experience filing Form 5471 for a solo service-based business? What information will you need from me to prepare it accurately?"
"As the sole U.S. shareholder, are there planning strategies we should consider, such as a Section 962 election, to manage my U.S. tax obligations more effectively?"

body.content: These are not the questions of someone lost in the dark. They are the questions of a confident global professional actively managing the financial engine of their business. You built a business to operate on a global scale; now you have the tools to ensure it thrives on a foundation of sound compliance and true peace of mind.
